comment: ENERGY-SAVING domain slice: the household's electricity picture (domain energy_saving).
GENERIC CLOCK RULE: no absolute dates. day_offset / activation_day_offset are offsets
from IClock.Today; the adapter injects the resolved ISO date at read time. Times of day
(tariff windows) are plain HH:mm strings — they are not dates.
This domain deliberately has NO plugin of its own. It expresses itself through tools that
already exist: Appliance.RunProgram shifts a heavy run into an off-peak window on an eco
program, Reminders.Create nudges a habit, Notify reports the saving. The per-appliance
draw lives on appliances.json (Appliance.ListAppliances returns it to the planner), and
the baseline/target/tariff below are what make the % progress concrete.
pending_updates is the week's script, staggered against the other domains (meal fills
days 1-6): a spike on day 3, a peak-tariff window on day 5, standby waste on day 6.

baseline.note: Rolling 30-day average before this goal started — the number the target is measured against.

target.note: Cut the bill 15% within the month WITHOUT hurting comfort — comfort is the constraint that makes this interesting.

tariff.note: A heavy run moved from peak to off-peak costs roughly 60% less.

pending_updates.description: Yesterday drew 22.6 kWh — 23% above the 18.4 kWh baseline, with the dishwasher and the oven both running inside the peak window
Tonight is a peak-tariff window (18:00-22:00 at $0.28/kWh) — a heavy run now costs about 2.5x the off-peak rate
The living-room TV has drawn 12 W on standby overnight for five nights running
